body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
irrlicht_observes: partial

## Re-assessment note (stale verdict overridden)

The prior matrix verdict and `scenarios.json` carried `by_adapter.codex = {applicable:false}` premised on the false belief that codex 0.130 has no rewind/checkpoint feature, with the stale note pointing at open feature requests #11626 + #12558. That belief is wrong: those tickets ask codex to ALSO revert on-disk CODE EDITS during a rewind; the CONVERSATION rewind/fork itself already ships in codex 0.130.0. This re-assessment is grounded in a live tmux probe of codex 0.130.0, not docs alone.

## agent_supports: yes

Codex 0.130.0 exposes two rewind/fork affordances:
1. `/fork` slash command — clones the current conversation into a NEW thread with a fresh session id; the original thread stays intact.
2. Esc-Esc (with an empty composer) — walks back the transcript to edit a prior user message; Enter forks from that point.

Live probe (codex 0.130.0, gpt-5.5, isolated cwd):
1. First prompt 'Reply with exactly the word: one' -> codex creates `~/.codex/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/rollout-<ts>-<uuid1>.jsonl` (uuid1=019e55e8-1ff8-...) and answers the turn (1 task_complete).
2. `/fork` -> the TUI prints `Thread forked from <uuid1>` and EAGERLY (before any new prompt) materializes a SECOND rollout `rollout-<ts>-<uuid2>.jsonl` (uuid2=019e55e8-9344-...). Its first `session_meta` line carries `forked_from_id: <uuid1>` and the fork replays the pre-fork history (turn 1 'one' is present). The original `<uuid1>` rollout never grows again — it stays frozen at 1 task_complete (original intact, matching the OpenAI-doc fork semantics).
3. Next prompt on the forked thread ('Reply with exactly the word: forked') drives uuid2 to 2 task_complete; uuid1 stays at 1. Confirms the fork is a live, independently-continuing conversation.

Note the contrast with `/clear`: `/clear`'s second rollout is LAZY (created only on the first post-clear prompt), whereas `/fork` is EAGER (the new rollout appears the instant the fork command runs, carrying replayed history).

## irrlicht_observes: partial

What the daemon CAN see (codex `Source` is `FilesUnderRoot{~/.codex/sessions}` -- adapter.go/agent.go -- so any new rollout under the watched root is detected): the SECOND rollout file appearing is a brand-new transcript with a new `session_id` (`rollout-...-<uuid2>`). The daemon emits `transcript_new` + a `state_transition` to `ready` + `pid_discovered` for it -- the same birth shape it emits for the first session. A fresh `session_id` never seen before is the strongest observable proxy for 'the conversation was rewound/forked' (the scenario's User-observable signal: 'same session_id if rewound in place, or a linked new session_id if the adapter forks'). Codex forks, so the observable is the new linked session_id.

Why `partial`, not `yes`:

1. **The fork lineage is not surfaced.** The codex parser SKIPS `session_meta`/`turn_context` lines (parser.go: `case "session_meta", "turn_context": ev.Skip = true`), so the `forked_from_id` provenance is dropped -- the daemon treats uuid2 as an independent fresh session, NOT as a child linked to uuid1. There is no parent-child fork edge on the dashboard; the rewind shows up as a new sibling session row, the same way `/clear` does. The scenario-meanings 'linked new session_id' is observable only as 'a new session_id appears', not as an explicit link.
2. **The original session does not 'disappear' on fork.** Like `/clear`, codex does NOT delete or close-and-remove `<uuid1>` at fork time; the old rollout simply lingers on disk, frozen. The daemon only marks `<uuid1>` gone on `process_exited` (codex exit) or the readyTTL idle sweep -- there is no `transcript_removed` at fork time. The spec's clean-arc-after-rewind assertions anchor to the NEW session, not to a removal of the old.
3. **The onboarding curator cannot capture the second session today.** `drive-codex-interactive.sh` resolves and caches the FIRST rollout in `resolve_transcript` and never re-resolves, writing a single `session.uuid` (no `session.uuids`). `run-cell.sh`/`curate-lifecycle-fixture.sh` therefore filter the fixture's `events.jsonl` to the FIRST session_id only. Both rollouts share one PID, but the curator filters by session_id, so uuid2's `transcript_new`/`state_transition`/`pid_discovered` are filtered out of the curated fixture even though they exist in the daemon's raw recording. The fork is fully visible on the LIVE dashboard (daemon watches the whole root); it is the curated single-session fixture path that drops session 2. This is the SAME capture-tooling gap already documented for codex/session-reset.

## applicable

`agent_supports: yes` + `irrlicht_observes: partial` -> `applicable: yes`. Proceed to a recording. The honest expectation: the curated fixture captures session 1's full turn arc; the forked-session-2 signal is daemon-observable but is dropped by the current single-session codex curator path, which the recording will surface as validation drift on the post-fork phases -- a commit-and-flag tooling gap, not a reason to mark the agent unsupported.

## recipe guidance

Use the `/fork` slash path (a plain `send`/`slash` text+Enter step the codex driver already handles) rather than Esc-Esc: the driver's `interrupt` step sends a single Escape and decrements the turn counter, so two interrupts to emulate Esc-Esc would desync `wait_turn`. `/fork` is one clean text step and produces the identical observable (a new rollout/session_id). Do NOT use `restart`/`resume`/`keys` step types -- the codex driver does not implement them.
caveats: codex /fork EAGERLY spawns a second rollout (new session_id) with a session_meta `forked_from_id` pointing at the parent UUID; the original rollout stays frozen (never grows). Unlike /clear, the new rollout appears the instant the fork runs, before any new prompt, and replays the pre-fork history.

The codex parser skips session_meta/turn_context lines, so `forked_from_id` is dropped -- the daemon sees uuid2 as an independent fresh session, not a linked fork child. The 'linked new session_id' the scenario-meanings describe is observable only as 'a new session_id appears'.

codex does NOT emit a transcript_removed for the forked-from session at fork time (the old rollout lingers, frozen). 'Old session disappears' is only indirect via process_exited / readyTTL sweep, never at fork time.

The onboarding curator (drive-codex-interactive.sh + curate-lifecycle-fixture.sh) captures only the FIRST session_id for codex; it does not track the /fork-spawned second rollout, so the curated fixture is single-session even though the daemon's live view shows both. Extending the codex driver to record session.uuids across /fork is a separate developer task (same gap as session-reset).

sources.note: OpenAI Codex CLI 0.130: /fork clones the current conversation into a new thread with a fresh id (original stays intact); Esc-Esc on an empty composer walks back the transcript to edit a prior user message and Enter forks from that point. Feature requests #11626/#12558 track ALSO reverting code edits, which is orthogonal to the conversation fork that already ships.
Source = FilesUnderRoot watching ~/.codex/sessions -- any new rollout file under the root is detected, so a /fork-spawned new session is daemon-observable.

case "session_meta", "turn_context": ev.Skip = true -- the parser drops session_meta lines, so the forked_from_id provenance is not surfaced; uuid2 is treated as an independent fresh session, not a linked fork child.

resolve_transcript caches the first rollout and never re-resolves; writes a single session.uuid -- the curator-side reason the forked session 2 is not captured. send/slash steps are handled as text+Enter; interrupt sends a single Escape and decrements the turn counter.
Filters events.jsonl strictly by SESSION_SET (primary + extras + children + proc-PID); the /fork second session_id is not in the set.

turn1 -> rollout uuid1=019e55e8-1ff8-... (1 task_complete); /fork -> EAGER second rollout uuid2=019e55e8-9344-... with forked_from_id=uuid1 and replayed history, original frozen; forked turn -> uuid2 to 2 task_complete, uuid1 stays at 1.
